Summary:
The appellant appeals an order prohibiting it from continuing proceedings or taking further action against the respondents in respect of an incident that occurred in September 2015 during a hockey game. The appellant is a society under the Societies Act. It had begun proceedings under its bylaws to suspend or expel the respondents from the hockey league due to the conduct of one of the owners of the Surrey Knights, who was also the coach. The coach had previously incurred a lengthy suspension in proceedings brought by BC Hockey for the same conduct that initiated the appellant's proceedings. The hearing before the Governors of the appellant proceeded but the Governors refrained from deliberating pending resolution of the respondents' petition. The chambers judge concluded that the appellant breached the principles of procedural fairness by failing to give adequate notice and failing to provide an unbiased decision maker, and its conduct was oppressive and unfairly prejudicial justifying a remedy under s. 102 of the Societies Act. Before the order was entered, the appellant sought an order that the judge disqualify herself due to an apprehension of bias on the basis that her son had played in the league at the time her decision was made, or alternatively to reopen the hearing to address an issue that was not properly raised in the petition. The judge dismissed the application on both points.
Held: Appeal dismissed. The chambers judge made no errors of law or principle and there is no basis to interfere with her findings and conclusions. The judge found that the appellant had not provided adequate notice of the case against the respondents, as it was not made clear how the other owner of the Surrey Knights or the team itself were implicated in the proceedings. The fact that the Surrey Knights is owned by a partnership does not affect the analysis required in the context of a proceeding under the Societies Act. The judge also found that the appellant's president was a biased decision maker who should not have participated in a preliminary meeting of the Governors or rendered two important preliminary decisions, matters that were not cured by the hearing before the Governors. The judge did not err in considering the interests of the players in her analysis, as the issue was relevant to the relief claimed under the Societies Act, it was clearly raised in the hearing, and the parties had an opportunity to address it. Nor did the judge err in the remedy granted or in her conclusion that an informed, reasonable person would think the petition was decided fairly.
Reasons for Judgment of the Honourable Madam Justice Fisher:
[1] The Pacific Junior Hockey League (PJHL) appeals an order prohibiting it from continuing any proceedings or taking any further actions against the respondents, Surrey Knights Junior Hockey, John Craighead and Amar Gill, in respect of an incident that took place on September 24, 2015.
[2] John Craighead is the coach, general manager, and one of the owners of the Surrey Knights Junior Hockey team (formerly the Langley Knights). Amar Gill is the other owner. The incident of September 24, 2015 arose after a fight broke out on the ice during a hockey game between the Langley Knights and the Mission City Outlaws. Mr. Craighead entered the Mission City bench and was subsequently accused of assaulting the assistant coach and a player.
[3] PJHL issued numerous penalties and suspensions to members of both teams, and suspended Mr. Craighead indefinitely. The police investigated the incident but no charges were laid. The British Columbia Amateur Hockey Association (known as BC Hockey) then conducted an investigation at the request of PJHL, and on January 30, 2016 suspended Mr. Craighead for six years. An appeal by Mr. Craighead to the BC Hockey Appeal Committee was dismissed in January 2017, but a further appeal in May 2017 to Hockey Canada resulted in his suspension being reduced to three years.
[4] Shortly after Hockey Canada's decision, PJHL initiated proceedings under its bylaws to suspend or expel the Surrey Knights from the league due to the conduct of Mr. Craighead. These are the proceedings that were the subject of the petition in the court below. In that petition, the respondents sought relief under s. 102 of the Societies Act, S.B.C. 2015, c. 18, alleging that the PJHL proceedings had been conducted in a manner that was oppressive, unfairly prejudicial and contrary to the principles of natural justice. While the respondents raised many complaints about the conduct of PJHL, the chambers judge considered that two issues of procedural fairness--sufficiency of notice and the right to be tried by an unbiased tribunal--were sufficient grounds on which to grant orders to cease the current and any future proceedings relating to the September 24, 2015 incident.
[5] After the reasons for judgment were released on October 12, 2018, PJHL applied to have the chambers judge disqualify herself due to a reasonable apprehension of bias, or alternatively, to reopen the hearing to argue an issue that it asserted was not properly raised in the petition. The judge dismissed both applications.
[6] In this appeal, PJHL asserts that the chambers judge made numerous errors, including misapplying the legal principles governing procedural fairness, granting a remedy with no connection to the errors identified, and refusing to recuse herself and set aside the October 12, 2018 judgment.
[7] To provide context for the factual background and the judgment below, I will first set out the relevant legislation and bylaws of PJHL.
The Societies Act and PJHL bylaws
[8] The respondents sought, and the chambers judge granted, relief from oppression under s. 102 of the Societies Act:
102 (1) A member of a society may apply to the court for an order under this section on the grounds that
(a) the activities or internal affairs of the society are being or were conducted, or the powers of the directors are being or were exercised, in a manner oppressive to the member or to the member and one or more other members, or
(b) an act of the society was done or is threatened, or a resolution of the members or directors was passed or is proposed, that is unfairly prejudicial to the member or to the member and one or more other members.
[9] There is a wide range of remedies available under s. 102(2), which the court may grant "with a view to remedying or bringing to an end the matters complained of". Such orders include directing or prohibiting any act, regulating the conduct of the society's activities or internal affairs, or directing the society to compensate an aggrieved person.
[10] PJHL is a society as defined in the Societies Act, and a member of BC Hockey, the body responsible for governing amateur hockey in the province. PJHL is also governed by its own constitution and bylaws. The bylaws deal with various matters including membership, management, and procedures for discipline and for suspending or expelling members.
[11] A member of PJHL is defined in the bylaws as either an "Operating" or "Honourary" member, but an Operating Member is not further defined. Any "person, firm, association, or corporation of good repute and financial responsibility" is eligible to be an Operating Member. Upon acceptance as a member of PJHL, an Operating Member is granted a franchise for the operation of a hockey club. An "Associated Person" is defined as "any Shareholder, Officer, Director, Governor, Alternate Governor, Manager, Coach, Player or other agent of an Operating Member".
[12] The PJHL is operated by governors and officers of the society. "Governors" are individuals appointed by the members to represent them, and the "Board of Executives" is the Governors and Officers who are considered to be the directors of the society "for the purposes of the Societies Act".
[13] Operating Members and Associated Persons are subject to disciplinary action by the PJHL president under Bylaw 14. One of the grounds for such action is where an Operating Member or Associated Person "conducted themselves in a manner prejudicial to the welfare of the Society or the public image of the Society or prejudicial to the welfare of any Member of the Society or to the conduct of amateur hockey". The president has the authority to impose suspensions, fines or other conduct directives, both interim and final. There is a right of appeal from a decision of the president to an Appeals Committee, and a further right of appeal to the Board of Executives.
[14] Only Operating or Honourary Members are subject to suspension or expulsion by the Governors under Bylaw 12. One of the grounds for suspension or expulsion, under Bylaw 12.1(h), is where the member "engages in conduct prejudicial to the welfare of the Society or prejudicial to the public image of the Society". Any Governor or Officer may "prefer" a charge against a member by filing this with the president. The president is required to convene a conference call of the Governors for the purpose of deciding whether the charge is to be dismissed or to proceed to a hearing. There is a right of appeal from a decision of the Governors to BC Hockey and a further right of appeal to Hockey Canada.
Factual background
[15] In this case, Mr. Craighead was subject to discipline in respect of the September 24, 2015 incident, not by PJHL under Bylaw 14, but by BC Hockey. This is because BC Hockey reserves to itself the ability to impose discipline on any "Registered Participant" for more serious matters such as those where a suspension may extend for a period greater than 60 days. A "Registered Participant" as defined in the BC Hockey bylaws includes a person registered with a member (such as PJHL) or affiliated with a club or team.
[16] I do not intend to review the details of the September 24, 2015 incident. Suffice it to say, following what can be described as a brawl on the ice, Mr. Craighead entered the bench of the opposing team. He was accused of assaulting the assistant coach and a player, an accusation that he denied, especially in respect of the player. In the days after the incident, players from both teams were assessed multi-game suspensions. Mission City's coach was suspended for ten games and the team was fined $2,500. Pending the outcome of a criminal investigation, PJHL issued an indefinite suspension to Mr. Craighead. At the time, Mr. Craighead was receiving a salary from the Surrey Knights as both coach and general manager.
BC Hockey investigation and appeal process
[17] In early December 2015, the police informed Mr. Craighead that criminal charges would not be laid against him. Ray Stonehouse, the president of PJHL, then asked BC Hockey to investigate Mr. Craighead's conduct regarding the incident. BC Hockey representatives interviewed Mr. Craighead and six other individuals. On January 30, 2016, BC Hockey suspended Mr. Craighead for a period of six years from the date of the incident. The notice of suspension provided the following reasons:
Leaving your bench after all your players joined an on ice altercation
Entering bench of an opposing team
Assaulting a Mission Assistant Coach
Striking a Mission Player
The actions taken by you on September 24, 2015 contravene BC Hockey's expectation of our coaches and the responsibility to provide and foster a safe environment for our players, coaches, officials, parents and fans.
Under BC Hockey's suspension guidelines, you will not be permitted to participate in any capacity in exhibition, jamboree, tournament, league, or play-off games, nor shall you act in any official capacity within BC Hockey or affiliated organizations until the term of this suspension has passed.
[18] Under BC Hockey's bylaws, Mr. Craighead appealed this decision to the BC Hockey Appeal Committee. On July 21, 2016, this appeal was dismissed as abandoned because it could not be scheduled within the timelines under the bylaws. Mr. Craighead successfully appealed this to Hockey Canada, which directed that the appeal be heard. Just before the appeal hearing in January 2017, BC Hockey sought to increase the length of the suspension on the basis that Mr. Craighead had continued to participate in hockey operations despite the suspension. On February 2, 2017, the Appeal Committee dismissed the appeal. It declined to increase the suspension in the absence of a "full investigation" into the allegations of non-compliance but upheld the six-year suspension, finding it reasonable on the facts that were found.
[19] Mr. Craighead successfully appealed this decision to a Special Committee of Hockey Canada. On May 27, 2017, the Special Committee reduced the suspension to two years as owner and three years as coach on the following terms:
A) -Mr. Craighead's suspension from all game related functions (including but not limited to coaching, managing, acting as a general manager, game day operations, timekeeper, or in any capacity, whether paid, volunteer or otherwise that is associated directly with the game on the ice or in the arena facility) remains in effect until Sept. 1, 2018;
B) -Mr. Craighead's involvement solely as an owner of a hockey team may commence effective Sept. 1, 2017.
C) -Mr. Craighead's interaction with and involvement in the Pacific Junior Hockey League is at the sole discretion of the PJHL.
[20] The Special Committee found the actions of Mr. Craighead to warrant a significant suspension, but expressed concerns about the delay in the investigation by BC Hockey and the lack of reasons for its decision:
The Committee was however, very concerned by the manner in which this incident was handled from the beginning. From the materials provided, it appears that while Mr. Craighead was initially suspended, pending a hearing, no action was taken to investigate this matter until a number of months later, after the police investigation was concluded, with no charges being laid. It was then investigated and the suspension for a period of six years was imposed but no details were provided at that time to support or explain the basis or factors upon which the suspension was based. There were no references to other incidents of a similar nature or the nature of suspensions imposed in other cases, to act as a guide in applying one of the principles of natural justice that similar punishment should be applied to those who commit infractions in similar circumstances. After communicating to Mr. Craighead that his appeal from that suspension would be heard, many months later the BCH Appeals Committee refused to hear the original appeal and referred Mr. Craighead to Hockey Canada to seek relief. Only after the Special Committee sent the matter back to [BC Hockey] on November 2, 2016, with a clear direction to it to conduct an appeal, was any explanation or justification for the length and nature of the suspension provided to anyone, including Mr. Craighead. This was approximately eleven or twelve months after the suspension was imposed.
[21] The Special Committee found this to be unacceptable and not in accordance with the principles of natural justice, as it was unable to review the basis on which the suspension had been imposed. The Committee was therefore required to assess an appropriate penalty itself from the materials filed in the appeal.
PJHL suspension and expulsion process
[22] Shortly after this decision was released, PJHL moved to suspend or expel the Surrey Knights under Bylaw 12. This process was instigated by a "charge" made by PJHL's vice-president, Jim Eaton (the Eaton complaint). In a letter to the PJHL president dated June 12, 2017, Mr. Eaton expressed the view that the Surrey Knights had engaged in conduct prejudicial to the welfare or the public image of PJHL, and he asked Mr. Stonehouse to place before the Governors "the question of whether the conduct of the Surrey Knights" violated Bylaw 12.1(h). The conduct he referred to was the incident of September 24, 2015 and the subsequent process by which BC Hockey suspended Mr. Craighead for six years. He then referred to the decision of Hockey Canada:
More recently, Hockey Canada has reduced the suspension such that:
- Mr. Craighead is suspended from all game related functions until September 1, 2018;
- Mr. Craighead is suspended from all involvement solely as an owner of a hockey team until September 1, 2017; and
- Mr. Craighead's interaction with and involvement in the PJHL is at the sole discretion of the PJHL.
[23] Mr. Eaton added an allegation that Mr. Craighead had not observed the suspension imposed on him, stating:
He has regularly observed practices and tryouts, interacted with and given instructions to the coach, and has participated in at least one team meeting.
[24] The basis for the "charge" was:
Mr. Craighead remains as the majority owner of the Surrey Knights, and the connection between the PJHL and the Surrey Knights continues to negatively impact the public image of the PJHL.
I believe that for all of these reasons, the Governors should seriously consider whether the Surrey Knights should be suspended or expelled from the PJHL.
I would ask that you accept this letter as a report for purposes of Bylaw 12.2(a) and advance this report through the process set out in balance of Bylaw 12.2.
[25] Counsel for the Surrey Knights objected to this process. In a letter dated June 20, 2017, Mr. Gall asserted that the matter had already been resolved, there was no reasonable basis to ground any action against the Surrey Knights, and the PJHL's process was a collateral attack on the decision of Hockey Canada. He sought a summary dismissal of the matter, and alternatively, particulars of the complaint.
[26] Mr. Stonehouse then convened a teleconference of the Governors to decide whether to dismiss the charge or call a meeting of the Board of Executives to hear the matter, as required under Bylaw 12.2(d). On July 4, 2017, the Governors voted 9-1 to proceed to a hearing of what Mr. Stonehouse confirmed to be "the charges against the Surrey Knights and majority owner John Craighead". The sole negative vote was that of Mr. Gill. On July 10, 2017, Mr. Stonehouse advised Mr. Gall that "the hearing into the complaint against Mr. John Craighead" would take place on August 1, 2017.
[27] On July 11, 2017, counsel for PJHL wrote to Mr. Stonehouse, copying Mr. Gall, about the anticipated evidence to be presented at the hearing and the nature of the proceeding. He attached sworn statements of seven individuals, including Mr. Stonehouse himself, and set out the decision the Governors would be called on to consider:
1. whether the Surrey Knights (formerly known as the Langley Knights) have engaged in conduct that is either:
a) prejudicial to the welfare of the PJHL; or
b) prejudicial to the public image of the PJHL;
and, if so:
2. whether the conduct justifies either a suspension of the membership of the Surrey Knights in the PJHL or, alternatively, an expulsion of the Surrey Knights from membership in the PJHL.
[28] Counsel then set out his position that the appropriate disposition was to expel the Surrey Knights from membership in PJHL:
In this case, both the conduct of Mr. Craighead during the game on September 24, 2015 and his subsequent actions in ignoring - or seeking to circumvent - the suspension imposed on him provide ample justification for a decision to expel the Surrey Knights from membership. Mr. Craighead's unwillingness to accept responsibility for his actions and his ongoing efforts to circumvent the suspension imposed on him provide further evidence that the actions of Mr. Craighead, and the team of which he is a majority owner, are prejudicial to both the welfare of and the public image of the Pacific Junior Hockey League.
[29] On July 14, 2017, Mr. Stonehouse advised all counsel that he intended to recuse himself from presiding over the August 1, 2017 hearing in light of the fact that he had sworn an affidavit setting out his "observations of some of the events that are central to the charges against Mr. Craighead". He therefore arranged for the Executive Vice President of PJHL to preside over the hearing. Nevertheless, he dealt with two preliminary matters related to the hearing, the first on a question of jurisdiction and the second regarding disclosure of documents.
[30] On July 19, 2017, Mr. Gall raised a preliminary objection that PJHL had no jurisdiction "to conduct a disciplinary hearing regarding Mr. Craighead" as the matter of his punishment had already been determined by Hockey Canada. He raised issues of res judicata, double jeopardy and collateral attack. He requested that PJHL schedule a preliminary hearing to address this objection.
[31] On July 21, 2017, Mr. Stonehouse denied Mr. Gall's request for a preliminary hearing, stating he was "not persuaded" that PJHL lacked jurisdiction. His reasons, which were subsequently clarified to some extent, can be summarized as follows:
· Mr. Craighead had been suspended by "an entirely separate body" and PJHL was not a party to that proceeding;
· the misconduct alleged in this proceeding was "substantively different" from the previous proceedings due to evidence being advanced that Mr. Craighead had contravened the terms of the suspension; and
· the principle of res judicata did not apply given the different parties and different objectives of the PJHL hearing, and double jeopardy did not apply because PJHL's authority was limited to suspending or terminating the Surrey Knights.
[32] He concluded with the following statement:
Notwithstanding everything that I have set out above, I wish to be very clear that you are not limited from arguing res judicata, double jeopardy or collateral attack at the hearing itself. While I do not see any grounds to adjourn the hearing or to have a prehearing on jurisdiction, you are entirely at liberty to advance such arguments as you feel are in your client's interests at the hearing on August 1
[33] Following this, Mr. Gall made a request for the production of three categories of documents: (1) communications between PJHL and BC Hockey regarding the September 24, 2015 incident; (2) documents in respect of investigations and disciplinary action taken by PJHL or Hockey Canada against other persons in relation to the same incident; and (3) documents, including internal correspondence, relating to Mr. Craighead or the Surrey Knights franchise. Mr. Stonehouse agreed to provide documents that included "evidence of the events which form the basis of the allegations against your client" but refused to do so in respect of the third category sought. He expressed the view that "the foundation for such a broad and all-encompassing request has not been made out and amounts to a fishing expedition".
[34] The hearing commenced on August 1, 2017. Mr. Gall first sought to proceed with his document and jurisdiction applications, but both were dismissed. The chair indicated that he agreed with Mr. Stonehouse's position with respect to the document production, and he said this regarding the jurisdiction issue:
In the application brought relative to jurisdiction, it is my understanding from a review of the correspondence exchanged between Mr. Gall and Mr. Stonehouse that the issue of jurisdiction has already been raised as a preliminary matter.
Mr. Stonehouse responded to Mr. Gall's submissions by finding that the PJHL does have jurisdiction to hold this hearing. Mr. Stonehouse also advised that Mr. Gall could advance an argument of res judicata, double jeopardy or collateral attack as part of his submissions. I am going to ask Mr. Gall to make that argument of his submissions at the conclusion of this case but I am not going to allow a further preliminary application in that regard at this time.
[35] The hearing was conducted over six evenings and concluded on August 30, 2017. The respondents filed their petition on August 21, 2017. By agreement of the parties, the Governors did not begin their deliberations, pending determination of the petition.
The judgments below
The petition -- October 12, 2018 reasons
Factual issues
[36] The chambers judge reviewed the factual background, essentially as I have described above. She made particular note of differing descriptions of the Eaton complaint as concerning "the Surrey Knights and the conduct of majority owner John Craighead" in Mr. Stonehouse's communications of June 24 and July 5, 2017 (at paras. 36 and 38). She found that PJHL counsel's July 11, 2017 letter characterized the purpose of the hearing to determine membership and whether Mr. Craighead's conduct warranted expulsion from the league of the Surrey Knights as well as Mr. Craighead (at para. 42).
[37] The judge then focused on three aspects of the hearing that she considered relevant to the issues before her--the opening statement of the chair, the evidence of Mr. Stonehouse, and the closing address of counsel for PJHL--and made some findings on that evidence.
[38] The judge noted that the opening statement of the chair focused solely on Mr. Craighead and said nothing about an obligation to prove the charges against the Surrey Knights or that the team itself was at risk of expulsion. She noted in particular the chair's introductory remarks and his description of the procedure:
[57] When introducing the participants, he stated:
To my right is Ian Hayward. He is legal advisor to the PJHL for this hearing. To his right is Bill Veenstra. He is a lawyer prosecuting the case against Mr. Craighead on behalf of the PJHL.
...
To my left is Peter Gall. He is a lawyer for Mr. Craighead. To his left is Mr. John Craighead, and he is a majority owner of the Surrey Knights and is the respondent in this hearing.
[58] Mr. McNeely then described the procedure and ended his opening remarks with these statements:
Once all evidence has been received, Mr. Gall will be entitled to make closing submissions on behalf of Mr. Craighead. Mr. Veenstra will then make his closing submissions. Once all submissions have been made, I will then call upon the governors to vote on whether or not the charges against Mr. Craighead have been proven, either in whole or in part, pursuant to bylaw 12-2(g). A vote accepting that the charges have been proven in whole or in part will only pass if two-thirds of the governors case their votes in favour of such a finding.
Neither Mr. Gill nor Mr. Craighead of the Surrey Knights will be entitled to cast a vote and so at least seven of the remaining ten governors must vote in favour of such a finding if it's to pass.
...
Ultimately, you will be asked to determine the following two questions: Has Mr. Veenstra proven that Mr. Craighead has engaged in the conduct alleged by the complainant in whole or in part; and two, do you find that such conduct is prejudicial to the welfare of the PJHL or that it is prejudicial to the public image of the PJHL?
You must answer yes to both of these questions to find against Mr. Craighead.
[Emphasis in original.]
[39] The judge noted as well that the opening statement of PJHL's counsel also focused on the conduct of Mr. Craighead and did not explain the basis on which the Surrey Knights could be found to have engaged in conduct prejudicial to PJHL so as to justify the team's expulsion from the league (at para. 63).
[40] The chambers judge then described some of the evidence given by Mr. Stonehouse regarding his belief that an assault had taken place and what witnesses he interviewed, why PJHL delayed these proceedings, and his role in the Governors' preliminary vote on whether to proceed to a hearing. She made certain findings about this evidence:
[66] The gist of this testimony was that Mr. Stonehouse absolutely believed that Mr. Craighead was guilty of assaulting two people because Mr. Stonehouse himself had witnessed it. As such, he believed it was unnecessary to conduct interviews of anyone who held a view different than his.
[68] Here, Mr. Stonehouse explains the PJHL's delay in initiating the proceedings against Mr. Craighead. Mr. Stonehouse did not answer the question about the impact of the delay on the team.
[70] Mr. Stonehouse did not render a vote. However, he was involved with the steering committee that brought the Eaton Complaint to the Governors who voted in favour of proceeding to a full hearing. Again, there is no record of what transpired during this conference call and prior to the Governors' vote.
[41] The judge also found that PJHL counsel's closing address made it clear that the culpability of the Surrey Knights was to be inferred through its association with Mr. Craighead, but the chair's final instructions to the Governors focused again "almost exclusively on Mr. Craighead, rather than the culpability of the team or the impact of a decision to expel the team" (at para. 72). She concluded that the message was then clear, in that "[t]he Governors could base their decision to expel the team solely on the basis of Mr. Craighead's conduct" (at para. 73).
[42] Finally, the chambers judge considered the evidence of Mr. Craighead and Mr. Gill regarding their financial commitments to the Surrey Knights and the likely implications to them and to their players if the Surrey Knights were expelled from the PJHL.
Legal issues
[43] The chambers judge then turned to the law regarding the court's limited supervisory role over voluntary associations, the requirements of procedural fairness and the oppression remedy under the Societies Act. She recognized that the court could only interfere to address procedural fairness concerns related to decisions of voluntary associations where legal rights were at stake, as discussed in Highwood Congregation of Jehovah's Witnesses (Judicial Committee) v. Wall, 2018 SCC 26. She framed the issues before her in accordance with Lakeside Colony of Hutterian Brethren v. Hofer, [1992] 3 S.C.R. 165 and recognized a distinction between procedural fairness requirements of a purely social club at one end of the spectrum and a trade union on the other, citing Barrie v. Royal Colwood Golf Club, 2001 BCSC 1181.
[44] The judge found that PJHL was not simply a social club, as the owners had a financial interest, the coach earned a livelihood by virtue of his employment, and individual players could lose their opportunity to play junior hockey should the team be expelled. On this basis she concluded that a more rigorous "natural justice" standard of procedural fairness was required, "significantly higher than that of a social club, though not as stringent as that of a trade union" (at paras. 86-87).
[45] While recognizing that the contents of the principles of natural justice are flexible, the chambers judge held that the "most basic requirements" were that of notice, opportunity to make representations, and an unbiased tribunal. On the issue of notice, she held that the Surrey Knights were entitled to be told "in substance what would happen" before they could "properly be adjudged guilty of an offence for which they could forever be expelled" (at para. 91). With respect to an unbiased tribunal, the judge applied the test set out in Newfoundland Telephone Co. v. Newfoundland (Board of Commissioners of Public Utilities), [1992] 1 S.C.R. 623 at 636: "whether a reasonably informed bystander could reasonably perceive bias on the part of an adjudicator" (at para. 97). She framed the issue before her as whether Mr. Stonehouse's preliminary decision making violated the unbiased tribunal requirement.
[46] The judge then considered the oppression provision in s. 102 of the Societies Act, and adopted the analysis in Dalpadado v. North Bend Land Society, 2018 BCSC 835. In Dalpadado, Brundrett J. considered the jurisprudence applicable to the similar corporate remedy, in particular BCE Inc. v. 1976 Debentureholders, 2008 SCC 69. He held that the two bases for oppression under the Societies Act are oppression under s. 102(1)(a) and unfair prejudice under s. 102(1)(b), and the inquiry must be concerned with "whether oppression or a harmful effect upon a member's legal or proprietary rights has been established" (at para. 107). He also grounded his analysis in the reasonable expectations of the members of the society. The chambers judge framed these issues as follows:
[108] In the circumstances presented here, the Petitioners bear the burden of establishing that the PJHL breached their reasonable expectations and that the conduct complained of amounts to "oppression" or "unfair prejudice." Breach of reasonable expectations is to be considered with regard to the stakeholders in the context and in regard to the relationships at play. The question is whether the expectation is reasonable having regard to the facts of the specific case, the relationships at issue, and the entire context, including the fact that there may be conflicting claims and expectations: BCE Inc. at para. 59 and 62.
Assessment and findings
[47] The chambers judge first addressed the issue of membership and concluded that all three respondents--the Surrey Knights (the franchise) and the owners (Mr. Craighead and Mr. Gill)--constituted an Operating Member under the PJHL bylaws. She then addressed the two issues of procedural fairness she considered necessary for her determination.
[48] First, the judge concluded that the Surrey Knights and Mr. Gill were not given adequate notice of the charge against them. She considered the request for particulars to be appropriate because the Eaton complaint provided only a description of the conduct of Mr. Craighead and nothing about how the conduct of the team or Mr. Gill warranted expulsion. She found it troubling that the request, which was never responded to, was handled by Mr. Stonehouse given his strongly held views about Mr. Craighead's culpability. She found that it was not until close to the end of the case that the Governors were told the team's liability rested exclusively on its association with Mr. Craighead.
[49] The chambers judge also concluded that PJHL violated the respondents' right to have an unbiased decision maker. In light of the history and Mr. Stonehouse's strong views about Mr. Craighead, she found his participation in the Governor's initial teleconference and in the preliminary decisions to constitute a breach of the requirements of procedural fairness.
[50] With respect to the teleconference, she reasoned as follows:
[124] Mr. Stonehouse knew he was to be a primary witness at the Hearing and that is why he recused himself as Chair. This was not a new circumstance that arose during the proceedings. Rather, the bias existed from the outset and Mr. Stonehouse acknowledged that was so.
[125] Despite this acknowledgment, Mr. Stonehouse convened the Governors to vote on whether the Eaton Complaint would proceed to a full hearing. This was involvement at a critical stage of the process -- it was an important decision. Although he did not render a vote personally, Mr. Stonehouse presided over the phone conference where the Governors voted 9-1 in favour of a full hearing.
[126] As President of the league, the Petitioners delivered the request for particulars to Mr. Stonehouse. He clearly had received it because he passed it along to the Governors in advance of their vote. Despite this, the vote occurred before the Surrey Knights were provided any particulars with respect to the charges against them. The PJHL Bylaws, per Bylaw 12.2(b), provide for the ability to respond to the charges (not just at the hearing but within seven days before the Governors are put to their preliminary vote), yet the vote occurred before this was possible. This vote was an important one and it needed to be administered by someone other than Mr. Stonehouse.
[127] Here, Mr. Stonehouse should not have participated in the phone conference. This was a significant step that required procedural safeguards. Simply put, it was unfair to the Surrey Knights for Mr. Stonehouse to preside over the Governors' vote.
[51] The judge considered Mr. Stonehouse's involvement in dealing with the preliminary applications to raise the most serious violations of procedural fairness. She found that the preliminary decisions were important to the hearing, Mr. Stonehouse should not have participated in them let alone render them, and to do so constituted a breach of the requirements of procedural fairness "that was not cured by the invitation to 'try again' at the end of the Hearing" (at para. 123).
[52] She found that the document request was not frivolous and went at least in part to the heart of the issue involving the team. She held that procedural fairness required someone other than Mr. Stonehouse to consider that request, as it was fundamentally unfair to the respondents that it was dealt with by a biased decision maker.
[53] With respect to Mr. Stonehouse's decision purporting to dismiss the respondents' application for a preliminary hearing on jurisdiction, the judge found that the chair relied on this prior decision in dismissing the same request at the beginning of the hearing, and offering to bring the application again at the end of the hearing did not fix the procedural unfairness. She therefore concluded:
[135] As such, I am satisfied that the Petitioners have demonstrated that Mr. Stonehouse's involvement in the preliminary Governors' vote, the application for disclosure and application to challenge jurisdiction, breached the requirements of procedural fairness. In other words, I am satisfied that a reasonably informed bystander would perceive bias in the circumstances presented here.
[54] Finally, the chambers judge found that the respondents had established a breach of their reasonable expectations and the conduct complained of was oppressive and unfairly prejudicial. More particularly, she found that the Surrey Knights reasonably expected that its players would be able to participate in a Junior B hockey league free of unfair interference from the PJHL and the PJHL would act in accordance with its bylaws and principles of natural justice. She found that Mr. Gill and Mr. Craighead would be impacted by expulsion by their significant financial interest, and the players on the team would be impacted either by being dispersed to another team or to no team at all. She held that the obligation of PJHL to act fairly and without oppression was an obligation owed to the operating member and not to just one of its owners. She concluded:
[143] I am satisfied based on the evidence presented that the PJHL engaged in conduct that was oppressive to the Surrey Knights, Mr. Gill and Mr. Craighead. In particular, I find that the PJHL's treatment of the Petitioners lacked probity and fair dealing. I make this finding based on the reasons set out above including the failure to provide sufficient notice and the failure to remove Mr. Stonehouse as a decision-maker. The lack of fair dealing is particularly evident with regard to the Surrey Knights and Mr. Gill. The PJHL did not recognize the interests of the team when it commenced the proceedings to expel. This was in circumstances where the team and Mr. Gill were at all times innocent of any wrongdoing and the PJHL never suggested otherwise. The PJHL's target was Mr. Craighead - this much was clear when I examine the events leading up to the hearing and the hearing itself. In addition to my findings regarding procedural fairness, the PJHL's decision to seek the expulsion of the team and Mr. Gill, in the circumstances presented here, was oppressive and contrary to section 102(1)(a) of the Societies Act.
[144] Further, I am satisfied based on the evidence presented that the PJHL engaged in conduct that was unfairly prejudicial to the Surrey Knights, Mr. Gill and Mr. Craighead. Here, I have considered the impact of the impugned conduct on each of the Petitioners. For the reasons set out above, I have found that the PJHL's decision moving to expel the team impacts not only the owners but also the players. As described by Mr. Gill, this is so despite the provisions in the PJHL Bylaws dealing with the dispersal of the team. I am satisfied that the Petitioners have established that the PJHL's conduct was unfairly prejudicial and constituted a violation of section 102(1)(b) of the Societies Act.
[145] Hockey Canada left it open for the PJHL to deal with Mr. Craighead. But when the league decided to target the operating member, it needed to ensure the rights and interests of the team, not just Mr. Craighead, were respected. The league failed in that regard. The operating member was entitled to proper notice. The team did not receive proper notice. The operating member was entitled to an unbiased decision-maker. It did not receive that either. I am satisfied that the conduct of the league was unfairly prejudicial and oppressive to the Petitioners.
[55] The chambers judge summarized her specific findings at para. 148:
a) The PJHL violated the principles of procedural fairness by instituting the proceedings against the Surrey Knights almost 21 months after the Incident and failed to provide adequate or sufficient notice to the Petitioners that their membership was at risk;
b) The PJHL violated the principles of procedural fairness that entitles a member to an unbiased tribunal. I base this finding on the fact Mr. Stonehouse himself rendered decisions on issues critical to the Petitioners' case and he did so in circumstances where he acknowledged his bias from the outset;
c) The conduct of the PJHL, in the manner which it targeted the Surrey Knights, the events leading up to the hearing (including Mr. Stonehouse's decision-making) and the manner in which it conducted the hearing, was conducted in manner oppressive to the Petitioners contrary to s. 102(1)(a) of the Societies Act; and
d) The conduct of the PJHL, in targeting the team, in addition to the other failures, was unfairly prejudicial to the Petitioners contrary to s. 102(1)(b) of the Societies Act.
[56] She therefore ordered PJHL to immediately cease any proceedings and take no further action against the respondents relating to the September 24, 2015 incident.
The reconsideration -- February 25, 2020 reasons
[57] In February 2020, before the order had been settled or entered, PJHL sought an order that the chambers judge disqualify herself from the proceeding and remit the petition back to the hearing list to be heard by another judge. It asserted a reasonable apprehension of bias on the basis that the judge's son was playing in the PJHL at the time the October 12, 2018 decision was rendered. Alternatively, PJHL sought an order to reopen the hearing to make further submissions on the impact of the Governors' decision on the individual players of the Surrey Knights, an issue PJHL asserted was not properly raised in the petition or addressed at the hearing. PJHL filed two affidavits providing some evidence on this issue.
[58] The circumstances relating to both applications were inter-related. PJHL contended that the chambers judge's findings in the October 12, 2018 reasons emphasized an impact on players, an issue it did not have the opportunity to address. It was because of this emphasis that PJHL raised its concerns about the judge's son playing in the league at that time.
[59] On the question of disqualification, the chambers judge reviewed the relevant ethical principles in the Canadian Judicial Council Catalogue and authorities from this court and the Supreme Court of Canada, including Liszkay v. Robinson, 2003 BCCA 506; Taylor Ventures Ltd. (Trustees of) v. Taylor, 2005 BCCA 350; Wewaykum Indian Band v. Canada, 2003 SCC 45 and Yukon Francophone School Board, Education Area #23 v. Yukon (Attorney General), 2015 SCC 25. She held that the criterion of disqualification was the reasonable apprehension of bias and the question was "what would an informed, reasonable and right-minded person, viewing the matter realistically and practically, and having thought the matter through, conclude regarding one of my children playing on a team in the League when I am determining a legal dispute between a different team in the League" (at para. 45). She determined that her son playing on a PJHL team was not enough to raise a reasonable apprehension of bias, taking into account evidence about the size of the league, the age group of the players eligible for participation, and the substantial turnover or transitory connection of the players to their teams. She could not see how "an informed, reasonable and right-minded person would think that the petition was not decided fairly" (at paras. 53 and 55).
[60] The chambers judge therefore concluded that she was not disqualified to hear and determine the petition, as no reasonable apprehension of bias had been established.
[61] With respect to the re-opening application, the chambers judge accepted that the court had inherent jurisdiction to reconsider a matter before entry of the relevant order, relying on various decisions of this court, including Grewal v. Grewal, 2016 BCCA 237 and Moradkhan v. Mofidi, 2013 BCCA 132.
[62] The judge first addressed whether it was probable that a miscarriage of justice would occur without a re-opening. She found that it would not, given the breadth of the petition, the time required for the hearing, the exchanges between counsel and the court on the issue, and the way in which PJHL framed the underlying complaint that gave rise to the court proceedings. She then considered whether a rehearing would probably change the result, and found that it would not. She considered it to defy reason for PJHL to argue, after judgment had been rendered, that the impact of an expulsion on the Surrey Knights' players was not an issue in the petition.
[63] She therefore dismissed the application to re-open.
On appeal
[64] The appellant asserts the following errors in judgment:
1. failure to consider the legal nature of the Surrey Knights as a partnership and its impact on the procedural fairness and oppression issues;
2. consideration of the interests of the players, when those interests had not been pleaded, were not the subject of a complete factual record, and are not a permissible consideration in an oppression proceeding;
3. misapplication of the legal principles governing procedural fairness issues
(a) by requiring the statement of legal propositions by way of "particulars";
(b) by assuming the existence of bias in preliminary rulings by Mr. Stonehouse, based on his decision not to be both witness and presiding officer at the hearing;
(c) in failing to consider whether there was any error in those preliminary rulings, and even if there was an error, whether that error was incapable of correction when final decisions were reached by the Governors;
4. granting a remedy without any logical connection to the procedural errors identified; and
5. failure to set aside the judgment and recuse herself from the hearing given the conflict issues that arose prior to judgment having been given.
[65] PJHL seeks an order allowing the appeal and dismissing the petition, or alternatively referring the matter back to the court below.
[66] The respondents submit that the orders below should be upheld on the procedural fairness basis as determined by the chambers judge. They also submit that in any event, there remained a number of alternative grounds and instances of unfairness set out in the petition that also justify the relief granted. Therefore, the respondents say that if this court concludes that the chambers judge made errors sufficient to undermine the result, it must consider whether those additional grounds provide a basis for the relief granted, or alternatively remit the matter back to the court below to decide anew.
Standard of review
[67] Orders made granting relief from oppression under the Societies Act are subject to the same standard of review as such orders made under comparable corporation statutes. Whether conduct amounts to oppression or unfair prejudice is a question of mixed law and fact, and in the absence of an extricable error of law, is reviewable on a palpable and overriding error standard. A finding of reasonable expectations is a finding of fact that is afforded similar deference. Remedies are discretionary, and owed deference unless they are based on errors of law, erroneous principles, irrelevant considerations, or are unjust: Khela v. Phoenix Homes Limited, 2015 BCCA 202 at paras. 37-38; Jaguar Financial Corporation v. Alternative Earth Resources Inc., 2016 BCCA 193 at paras. 63-64; Radford v. MacMillan, 2018 BCCA 335 at para. 54; and Wilson v. Alharayeri, 2017 SCC 39 at para. 59.
[68] PJHL does not assert any errors of law with respect to the oppression remedy or the principles of procedural fairness as they apply to voluntary associations. Although it asserts a misapplication of the principles of procedural fairness, the essence of the alleged errors relates to factual findings, findings of mixed fact and law, and the discretionary decision to grant relief under the Societies Act, all of which must be accorded deference by this court.
Analysis
[69] PJHL's submissions on appeal rely in large part on its primary position that the chambers judge did not consider and apply the legal consequence of the partnership relationship of Mr. Craighead and Mr. Gill as owners of the Surrey Knights. This forms the basis for its assertions that the respondents received adequate particulars and notice of the proceeding, the judge improperly considered the interests of the Surrey Knights' players and of Mr. Gill, and the remedy granted was too broad. As I explain below, I do not agree with PJHL that the existence of the partnership limits the analysis of the circumstances giving rise to allegations of oppression, unfair prejudice and breaches of natural justice in the administrative law context here.
1. Partnership
[70] PJHL submits that the chambers judge was not alive to the existence or legal significance of the partnership relationship of the Surrey Knights and its owners, Mr. Gill and Mr. Craighead. This is significant, it says, because a partnership is not separately distinguishable from its partners, and partners carry on business both as principals and agents of each other with respect to the partnership business. Since wrongful actions taken by one partner (i.e., Mr. Craighead) are imputably taken by all partners (i.e., Mr. Gill), PJHL contends that there was no basis for the chambers judge to find that particulars ought to have been provided, nor was there any basis for finding a lack of adequate notice.
[71] The respondents suggest that PJHL's submission misunderstands the decision below as well as the equitable purposes of the Societies Act. They submit that the question before the court was not one of the liability of a partnership but rather of what was appropriate, just, fair, and in accordance with the reasonable expectations of the parties.
[72] I agree with the respondents that the legal principles regarding the liability of a partnership are of little assistance in a case such as this. While PJHL has correctly described these general principles, the cases to which they refer, as well as the provisions of the Partnership Act, R.S.B.C. 1996, c. 348, relate primarily to a partnership's liability for debts, obligations or losses occasioned by a partner: see Partnership Act, ss. 7, 8, 11 and 12; Coal Harbour Properties Partnership v. Liu, 2004 BCCA 283 (obligation under a sale agreement); Strother v. 3464920 Canada Inc., 2007 SCC 24 (losses for breach of fiduciary duty); and Dubai Aluminum Company v. Salaam, [2003] 2 A.C. 366 (losses from a conspiracy to defraud).
[73] In my view, PJHL's strict reliance on a partnership relationship makes little sense in the context of the governance structure of PJHL. For example, Mr. Craighead was an owner, a coach and general manager of the Surrey Knights. His employment as a coach and general manager does not fall neatly within the partnership paradigm, since a partner cannot generally be an employee of a partnership: see McCormick v. Fasken Martineau DuMoulin LLP, 2014 SCC 39, aff'ing Fasken Martineau DuMoulin LLP v. British Columbia (Human Rights Tribunal), 2012 BCCA 313. However, as a coach and manager, Mr. Craighead was subject to PJHL bylaws as an "Associated Person" subject to discipline. In addition, eligibility to be an Operating Member includes individuals and corporations as well as partnerships. Clearly, the parties affected by decisions made by PJHL are varied and go beyond the strict confines of a partnership or any other ownership interest.
[74] More importantly, the fact that Mr. Craighead and Mr. Gill, as partners, own the Surrey Knights, cannot in itself govern the basis for PJHL to seek expulsion of the Surrey Knights from the league under its bylaws. I say this because an "Operating Member"--the entity that may be suspended or expelled under Bylaw 12--was defined by the chambers judge as including all three respondents, only two of whom are partners. While the issue of membership was the subject of rather extensive submissions in the court below, PJHL has not raised the judge's interpretation of membership as an issue in this appeal.
[75] Before the chambers judge, the issue of membership was raised in the petition as a question of jurisdiction. The respondents argued that PJHL did not have jurisdiction over the Surrey Knights because only the owners of a franchise constituted an "Operating Member". PJHL countered this with an argument that the Surrey Knights, as a "conglomerate" of constituent parts that included the owners as "Associated Persons", constituted an "Operating Member", and this definition excluded the individual owners and the franchise itself. More simply, PJHL argued that the partnership of Mr. Craighead and Mr. Gill was the "Operating Member", which controlled the Surrey Knights franchise, and Mr. Craighead was an "Associated Person". It asserted jurisdiction over the Surrey Knights under Bylaw 12 on the basis of the conduct of one of its "Associated Persons", i.e., Mr. Craighead.
[76] The chambers judge only briefly addressed this issue, at para. 112 of her October 12, 2018 reasons:
[112] I have reviewed the PJHL Bylaws and the applicable provisions of the Societies Act. Interpreting the PJHL Bylaws in accordance with their plain and ordinary meaning, I agree with the Respondent that the Surrey Knights, the franchise, and the owners, Mr. Gill and Mr. Craighead, constitute an Operating Member. Accordingly, I find that the PJHL had jurisdiction to conduct disciplinary proceedings against the Surrey Knights as prescribed by Bylaw 12.
[77] Whether or not the judge correctly captured PJHL's position, she grounded PJHL's jurisdiction in a definition of "Operating Member" that included the Surrey Knights franchise--i.e., the team--and its owners.
[78] Aside from the issue of jurisdiction (which is also not an issue in this appeal), it follows from the judge's definition of Operating Member that the issues in dispute were well beyond the liability of a partnership for the wrongful acts of a partner. They involved the interests of the Surrey Knights franchise and the owners in maintaining membership in the PJHL. I say this even though the judge did not refer to the fact of the partnership. Whether an Operating Member constitutes only the partnership itself or whether it includes the franchise, the franchise that is operated by the partnership clearly has an interest in its position of membership. The franchise is not a partner, so it cannot automatically follow that it is or should be "liable" for the wrongful acts of one of its owners. Moreover, to say that the franchise is something different from the team in this context makes no sense. In fact, in its factum, PJHL itself referred to the "actual members" of PJHL as the "teams" who are represented by the Governors.
[79] I would not give effect to this ground of appeal.
2. Interests of the players
[80] PJHL asserts that the determination of this proceeding on the basis of the impact or interests of the players constituted a misapplication of s. 102(1) of the Societies Act and a breach of natural justice.
[81] PJHL's submission on this issue presumes that only the partnership of Mr. Craighead and Mr. Gill is a "member" of PJHL for the purpose of the Societies Act, the players being distinct from "member". It contends that the chambers judge erred in considering the interests of the players not only because they were "non‑members" but also because this was not raised in the petition.
[82] Again, PJHL relies on the Surrey Knights being characterized only as a partnership:
The impact on players ultimately took a central role in the judgment of the Court - see in particular the specific references to player interests at RJ § 75, 86, 87, 140, 141 and 144 as well as the Chambers Judge repeatedly drawing distinctions between the team and Mr. Craighead [RJ § 54, 56, 59, 63, 68, 72, 73, 116, 119, 120, 121, 142, 143 and 145].
These many references throughout the Reasons for Judgment establish that the Chambers Judge considered the Surrey Knights to be an inchoate combination of Mr. Gill, Mr. Craighouse and the players, rather than the partnership that it actually was. This view of the team, and the importance assigned to the interests of the players, was key to the analysis of the Chambers Judge and, as will be discussed below, impacted on the Court's determination of the procedural fairness and oppression issues considered.
[Emphasis added.]
[83] I respectfully disagree with PJHL's submission on this point, as again, it is premised on what I see as a restrictive view of who is a member--either of the PJHL as a society generally or as an Operating Member under the PJHL bylaws. I also disagree that the impact on the players took a "central role" in the decision. The chambers judge properly considered the interests of all three entities she determined were affected by the PJHL proceedings--the Surrey Knights franchise or team, and the two owners. She made only one finding regarding the players themselves, that they could lose their opportunity to play junior hockey should the team be expelled, but her findings of reasonable expectations were appropriately focused on the expectations of the team itself. This is exemplified at para. 140 of her October 12, 2018 reasons:
[140] The Surrey Knights expected that its 16 to 20 year old players would be able to participate in a Junior B hockey league free of unfair interference from the PJHL. I find that this expectation was reasonable in the circumstances. Further, the Surrey Knights were entitled to expect the Society to act in accordance with its bylaws and principles of natural justice. Those requirements included the requirement that the team be provided sufficient notice should the PJHL seek to expel the team from the league. If a decision was made to expel, it was reasonable for the Surrey Knights to expect that decisions would not be made by a biased tribunal.
[84] I see no error in the chambers judge's conclusion in the reconsideration decision that the findings made in the October 12, 2018 decision necessarily engaged the interests of the players due to the fact that PJHL framed the underlying complaint as against the Surrey Knights team. As she stated in the February 25, 2020 reasons:
[68] The court made findings about the conduct of the League in reference to the petitioners. It is those findings that perhaps necessarily engage the interest of individual players, as it is difficult to separate the team from its players. However, the findings remain specific to the named petitioners.
[85] I also see no error in the judge's conclusion that the impact of an expulsion on the players was an issue in the petition. She carefully reviewed the record before her, including the extensive allegations in the petition, the evidence adduced, the exchanges between the court and counsel, and the final submissions. While the petition had a broad focus, the issue of impact was relevant to the relief claimed under the Societies Act, it was clearly raised in the hearing, and the parties had an opportunity to address it. This situation was not comparable to those in cases such as Canada Trustco Mortgage Co. v. Renard, 2008 BCCA 343, Hawkeye Power Corporation v. Sigma Engineering Ltd., 2012 BCCA 414, Xie v. Yuan, 2016 BCCA 238, or Labatt Brewing Co. Ltd. v. NHL Enterprises Canada L.P., 2011 ONCA 511.
[86] As for the evidence that was considered, PJHL pointed out that evidence about what would happen in a dispersal draft in the event of an expulsion was contained in an affidavit of Mr. Gill that had been prepared in support of an injunction application that never proceeded. Nonetheless, this affidavit found its way into the petition record and the evidence was referred to in the hearing without objection. Not only does this support the chambers judge's finding that the issue was raised, it also shows that PJHL had an opportunity to address it. In any event, PJHL tendered additional evidence from Mr. Stonehouse and from Barry Petrachenko, the chief executive officer of BC Hockey, in support of the reconsideration. The chambers judge found some of this evidence to be not substantially different from the evidence of Mr. Gill (regarding the notion of a dispersal draft), and some to be too speculative to be of much probative value (regarding the availability of purchasers for teams). Having reviewed that evidence, I see no basis to interfere with the chambers judge's findings on this point.
[87] Therefore, I would not accede to this ground of appeal.
3. Procedural fairness
[88] Where a legal right is at stake in relation to membership in a voluntary association, such as a property or civil right, courts have intervened to address procedural fairness concerns: Highwood Congregation at paras. 12 and 24; Hofer at 174. In this case, there are property and contractual rights at stake. Both Mr. Craighead and Mr. Gill have a financial investment in the Surrey Knights, and Mr. Craighead has contractual rights in respect of his employment as coach and general manager. The Surrey Knights team as a franchise is an asset, and the players of the team have a right that can be described as akin to a contractual one, given the commitments exchanged between the owners and the players regarding their right to participate in Junior hockey as members of the team.
[89] In this context, the right to notice of the cause for which a member of an organization is to be expelled, as well as the right to an unbiased tribunal to decide the issue, are fundamental requirements of natural justice and procedural fairness. Notice requires being informed of the nature of the charge and the consequences: Hofer at 195-196. The requirement for impartiality is based on an unbiased appearance, the test for which is whether a reasonably informed bystander could reasonably perceive bias on the part of a decision maker: Newfoundland Telephone at 636.
[90] As noted above, PJHL does not challenge the chambers judge's legal analysis with respect to the principles of procedural fairness as they apply to voluntary associations. PJHL submits that the judge ignored two fundamental issues: (a) whether the respondents were given a fair opportunity to present their position before the Governors, and (b) whether the respondents established that the Governors were not capable of fairly deciding the questions before them. It says that the judge failed to consider the interest of PJHL in making decisions about the needs of its organization and improperly limited her focus to the immediate needs of Mr. Gill and the players.
[91] I am unable to accept this submission. The chambers judge was faced with an extremely detailed petition that raised "a litany of complaints" about the conduct of PJHL. She did not consider it necessary to address all of them because she found two procedural issues to be fundamental to the process she was reviewing. In this context, it cannot be said that the judge failed to consider the interests of PJHL as an organization, and for the reasons I have rejected the first two grounds of appeal, it cannot be said that she improperly limited her focus as alleged.
Notice
[92] More specifically on the issue of notice, PJHL asserts that the chambers judge was wrong to conclude that it never responded to the request for particulars, or that notice of the basis for the team's liability was not provided until the close of the case before the Governors. It says that the letter from PJHL counsel of July 11, 2017 provided ample particulars of the charge, and the Eaton complaint itself, along with the July 11, 2017 letter, provided adequate notice of the case against the respondents.
[93] On this issue, the chambers judge reasoned as follows in the October 12, 2018 decision:
[119] Adequate and timely notice provides the member with an opportunity to consider its position and to govern itself accordingly, including preparing a defence. Under the PJHL Bylaws, the recipient of a complaint has a right to respond (in writing only and within seven days of receiving the complaint) before the Governors were put to a vote. Pursuant to Hofer and the authorities cited therein, the team, not just Mr. Craighead, was entitled to adequate and timely notice. The PJHL Bylaws provide the member with an opportunity to respond. Here, the team was not given that opportunity. If "guilt by association" was the PJHL's theory then the team should have been advised accordingly. The team was not. In essence, the team was deprived of the chance to answer the Eaton Complaint before the Governors' vote. I find that this failure was procedurally unfair to the Surrey Knights and Mr. Gill.
[120] The Hearing and the events preceding the Hearing focused on Mr. Craighead. The team and Mr. Gill were but an afterthought
[121] I find that the Eaton Complaint and the Hearing did not provide adequate notice to the Surrey Knights and Mr. Gill. If an operating member is to be expelled, the operating member (not just one of its owners) is entitled to notice. And fairness requires, particularly in the circumstances presented here, timely notice - to the team. [PJHL] explained the delay on the basis, in part, that they did not want to come after Mr. Craighead from all angles. However, that explanation ignores the interests of the team (separate and apart from those of Mr. Craighead). Until the delivery of the Eaton Complaint, the team had no idea that it was in any way at risk of being thrown out of the league. In the circumstances presented here, the notice given to the team was inadequate, fundamentally unfair, and constituted a breach of the principles of natural justice.
[Emphasis in original.]
[94] I see no error in the judge's findings or her reasoning. She was clearly assessing the issue of notice in the context of all of the evidence before her. That evidence demonstrated that PJHL was never clear on the process it was instituting.
[95] First, the Eaton complaint raised the question of "whether the conduct of the Surrey Knights violates Bylaw 12.1(h)". The conduct complained of, however, was only that of Mr. Craighead, and only in relation to the September 24, 2015 incident and the subsequent disciplinary proceedings against him. The sole allegation was that Mr. Craighead remained the majority owner of the Surrey Knights "and the connection between the PJHL and the Surrey Knights continues to negatively impact the public image of the PJHL". This is a very broad statement with no supporting foundation offered. If "guilt by association" was the basis for the charge, this ought to have been explicitly set out.
[96] Second, PJHL counsel's July 11, 2017 letter cannot be considered proper particulars. It simply attached copies of witness statements, explained the nature of the hearing and repeated the allegations regarding Mr. Craighead's conduct.
[97] Third, PJHL representatives appeared to view the proceedings as a complaint against only Mr. Craighead. This is reflected in Mr. Stonehouse's notice of the hearing date, communications from counsel for the chair in preparation for the hearing, and the chair's opening comments and instructions to the Governors at the hearing.
[98] Mr. Stonehouse's notice of July 10, 2017 described the hearing as "the hearing into the complaint against Mr. John Craighead". In preparation for the hearing, counsel appointed to advise the chair sought the agreement of counsel as to the exclusion of witnesses, which recognized the right of only Mr. Craighead to be present throughout. And perhaps most importantly, the chair of the hearing appeared to understand that only Mr. Craighead was the subject of the charges.
[99] All of this evidence was considered by the chambers judge. She commented specifically on the question of the exclusion of witnesses at para. 52:
[52] With respect to the suggestion that there be an order excluding witnesses, Mr. Hayward specifically recognized Mr. Craighead's right to be present throughout the Hearing. Despite also facing expulsion, it seems to have been overlooked that the team and Mr. Gill shared the same right to be present.
[100] She also set out the comments of the chair at the opening of the hearing:
[56] The Chair commenced the Hearing with introductions. In his opening remarks, Mr. McNeely makes a passing reference to Mr. Gill only to confirm that he did not have the right to vote. Similarly, Mr. McNeely makes a passing reference to the Surrey Knights only insofar as Mr. Craighead was the majority owner. Mr. McNeely did not otherwise mention the Surrey Knights or explain how it was that the Surrey Knights' conduct was disreputable. Mr. McNeely's opening remarks focused solely on Mr. Craighead.
[57] When introducing the participants, he stated:
To my right is Ian Hayward. He is legal advisor to the PJHL for this hearing. To his right is Bill Veenstra. He is a lawyer prosecuting the case against Mr. Craighead on behalf of the PJHL.
...
To my left is Peter Gall. He is a lawyer for Mr. Craighead. To his left is Mr. John Craighead, and he is a majority owner of the Surrey Knights and is the respondent in this hearing.
[58] Mr. McNeely then described the procedure and ended his opening remarks with these statements:
Once all evidence has been received, Mr. Gall will be entitled to make closing submissions on behalf of Mr. Craighead. Mr. Veenstra will then make his closing submissions. Once all submissions have been made, I will then call upon the governors to vote on whether or not the charges against Mr. Craighead have been proven, either in whole or in part, pursuant to bylaw 12-2(g). A vote accepting that the charges have been proven in whole or in part will only pass if two-thirds of the governors case their votes in favour of such a finding.
Neither Mr. Gill nor Mr. Craighead of the Surrey Knights will be entitled to cast a vote and so at least seven of the remaining ten governors must vote in favour of such a finding if it's to pass.
...
Ultimately, you will be asked to determine the following two questions: Has Mr. Veenstra proven that Mr. Craighead has engaged in the conduct alleged by the complainant in whole or in part; and two, do you find that such conduct is prejudicial to the welfare of the PJHL or that it is prejudicial to the public image of the PJHL?
You must answer yes to both of these questions to find against Mr. Craighead.
[Emphasis in original.]
[101] All of this evidence is underlined by a review of the transcript of the hearing itself, which was entirely focused on Mr. Craighead's conduct on and related to the September 24, 2015 incident. While I appreciate that Mr. Craighead always disputed the details of what occurred--in particular that he assaulted the Mission City player who also entered the bench--he ultimately accepted responsibility for his participation in the incident. Before the BC Hockey Appeal Committee, he sought a three-year suspension as reflecting his degree of culpability in the matter. On further appeal to Hockey Canada, a reduction of the suspension to two and three years was considered appropriate on the basis of the materials before it. Given this, I find it difficult to understand why it was necessary in the context of these proceedings to essentially re-litigate the details of the assault at all.
[102] The only additional matter that was raised in the Eaton complaint was the allegation that Mr. Craighead had violated the terms of his suspension by regularly observing practices and tryouts, interacting with and giving instructions to the coach and participating in "at least" one team meeting. The chambers judge was satisfied that this allegation did not preserve the proceedings given her conclusions on the requirements of procedural fairness (at para. 146). I would add that this allegation was also devoid of any particulars, it arose from an order of a different organization, BC Hockey, which never investigated the allegation, and the order was in any event varied by Hockey Canada. I would not therefore interfere with the chambers judge's conclusion on this point as well.
[103] Finally, the chambers judge made mention of PJHL's delay in instituting the proceedings under Bylaw 12. I do not read her reasons as including delay as a breach of procedural fairness on its own, but rather as an element of the lack of timely notice. The judge was clearly concerned that the team had no idea for almost 21 months after the incident that it was at risk of expulsion. She found Mr. Stonehouse's explanation that PJHL did not want to "be coming at" Mr. Craighead while BC Hockey was investigating the incident to ignore the interests of the Surrey Knights. I also see his explanation as another example of PJHL's confusion about who it was pursuing.
[104] In my view, this is a case where the governing body was unclear on the nature of its proceeding. In such circumstances, it is difficult to understand how a respondent could be said to have adequate notice of the case to meet.
Bias
[105] In its factum, PJHL submitted that the fact that Mr. Stonehouse was a witness to the September 24, 2015 incident did not make him biased. However, it did not press that point in oral argument, and I see no basis to interfere with the chambers judge's finding that the bias existed from the outset as acknowledged by Mr. Stonehouse. In any event, I understand that PJHL challenges the chambers judge's conclusion on the issue of an unbiased decision maker primarily on the basis that any involvement by Mr. Stonehouse prior to the hearing before the Governors--assuming he was biased--was cured by the hearing itself. It submits that none of the preliminary matters were truly important and there is no reason to suggest that the Governors are not capable of making decisions under Bylaw 12 in a fair and open-minded manner.
[106] PJHL relies on the principles set out in Histed v. Law Society (Manitoba), 2006 MBCA 89, which held that a reasonable apprehension of bias at an early investigative stage of proceedings can be cured by a subsequent hearing that adheres to the principles of natural justice (at para. 48).
[107] It is a general principle that any defects in an administrative process at an early stage of a proceeding--in particular an investigative stage--may be cured by a subsequent hearing. However, the nature of the initial process or hearing and the relevant statutory provisions are important factors when assessing whether a defect has been cured, and the subsequent hearing must provide a full and fair opportunity for the parties to be heard: see Histed at paras. 55-56, citing Ocean Port Hotel Ltd. v. British Columbia (General Manager, Liquor Control and Licensing Branch), 2001 SCC 52.
[108] Histed involved an apprehension of bias arising from the participation of several individuals at a meeting of the Complaints Investigation Committee of the Law Society, which authorized charges against a lawyer. Within the statutory framework, the Committee was authorized to take various actions, ranging from taking no further action to directing a charge to be laid, and its function in that case was not adjudicative. There was no complaint about the fairness of the subsequent hearing before the Discipline Committee. As the lawyer was given a full opportunity to answer the charges, any reasonable apprehension of bias that may have existed in relation to the Complaints Investigation Committee was cured by the subsequent hearing.
[109] The circumstances of this case were quite different from Histed. The chambers judge was concerned about Mr. Stonehouse's role in preliminary adjudicative, not investigative functions, in circumstances where he had acknowledged his conflict of interest. As noted above, she found that these preliminary decisions were important to the hearing process, and in light of the history of the matter and Mr. Stonehouse's "very strong views" about Mr. Craighead, he "should not have participated in these decisions let alone render them" (at para. 123). Importantly, she also found that this defect was not cured by the subsequent hearing given the chair's reliance on the prior decisions in dismissing the respondents' arguments on jurisdiction and document production at the start of the hearing.
[110] I see no basis on which to interfere with the judge's findings on these matters. The record clearly establishes that Mr. Stonehouse was not impartial given his very strong views about Mr. Craighead and his role in the September 24, 2015 incident. Knowing he was to be a witness at the hearing, he nonetheless convened the Governors to vote on whether the Eaton complaint should be dismissed or should proceed to a hearing. The judge viewed this conference call as an important vote that needed to be administered by someone other than Mr. Stonehouse. She was also concerned that the vote was held before the respondents were provided with any particulars of the charge. I do not accept PJHL's suggestion that there is nothing in the record to support the judge's finding that the request for particulars was handled by Mr. Stonehouse. The request was made in the June 20, 2017 letter from counsel for the respondents, addressed to Mr. Stonehouse, to which Mr. Stonehouse responded by simply delivering the Eaton complaint and the June 20, 2017 letter to the Governors and then convening the conference call.
[111] Earlier in her October 12, 2018 reasons, the chambers judge referred to the "poisoning the well" principle as reviewed in Sparvier v. Cowessess Indian Band No. 73, [1993] 3 F.C. 142 (at para. 98). This principle was succinctly summarized in David J. Mullan, Administrative Law, 2nd ed. (Irwin Law, 2001) at page 3-131 (cited in Sparvier):
A reasonable apprehension of bias in one member of a tribunal is sufficient to disqualify the whole tribunal, even though that member merely sat at the hearing without taking an active role in either it or subsequent deliberations. Mere presence is generally enough.
[112] The chambers judge did not go so far as to apply this principle to the Governors' vote, but she clearly considered Mr. Stonehouse's involvement in that meeting to be unfair to the Surrey Knights. Her finding that this was a significant step that required procedural safeguards is supported in the record. The fact that Mr. Stonehouse did not vote did not cure the unfairness, especially in the context here, where the only Governor to vote against a hearing was Mr. Gill--whose ability to vote is itself questionable given PJHL's assertion that the charges were against the partnership.
[113] All that said, the chambers judge viewed Mr. Stonehouse's involvement in the two preliminary applications to be the most serious violations of procedural fairness. Again, I see no error in her analysis.
