Both of these decisions were important to the conduct of the proceeding, and both were made after Mr. Stonehouse had removed himself from presiding over the hearing. In assessing a reasonable apprehension of bias, the question is not whether those decisions were reasonable, as submitted by PJHL. Procedural fairness is an essential aspect to a proceeding before an administrative body, and the damage created by an apprehension of bias cannot be remedied by a subsequent decision: Newfoundland Telephone at 645.
[114] Moreover, I do not accept PJHL's submission that the chambers judge erred in focussing almost entirely on the preliminary decisions and ignoring the hearing that subsequently unfolded. She did not ignore what occurred at the hearing. To the contrary, she considered the manner in which the chair addressed the jurisdiction and documents applications and found that the offer to raise the issues again at the conclusion of the hearing did not fix the procedural unfairness arising out of Mr. Stonehouse's active involvement in those preliminary decisions. These kinds of decisions are more properly addressed at the outset of a hearing, as their determination will affect either the outcome generally (as in questions of jurisdiction) or the conduct of the hearing (as in questions of document production).
[115] The chambers judge's conclusion that the apprehension of bias in these circumstances was not cured by the subsequent hearing before the Governors, especially when considered along with the lack of adequate notice, is amply supported in the record.
4. Remedy
[116] Section 102 of the Societies Act provides that a member of a society may apply to the court to remedy oppressive or unfairly prejudicial conduct by a society. This remedy is similar to that which is available to shareholders in the federal and provincial corporation statutes, and the same general principles, established in BCE Inc., are applicable. I agree with the analysis by Brundrett J. in Dalpadado regarding the application of these principles in the context of the remedy under s. 102. I would summarize the applicable principles as follows:
· section 102 gives a court a broad discretion to grant an equitable remedy to enforce what is not just legal, but fair;
· the inquiry into oppressive or unfairly prejudicial conduct is fact-specific and assessed on the basis of the reasonable expectations of the applicant member in the context of the relationships at play;
· factors relevant to determining reasonable expectations include the size, nature and structure of the society, the general practice in the business of the society, the relationship between the parties, past practice, steps the applicant could have taken to protect itself, representations and agreements made, and the fair resolution of conflicting interests between the society and its members;
· the applicant must establish harm to its peculiar legal or proprietary interests, distinct from the interests of other members: see Jaguar Financial Corporation at para. 179;
· if the applicant establishes a breach of reasonable expectations, the court must then determine whether the conduct complained of amounts to oppression or unfair prejudice; and
· while oppression connotes more serious conduct than unfair prejudice, bad faith need not be shown, as it is the effect rather than the subjective intent behind the impugned conduct that is key to a finding of oppression: see Icahn Partners LP v. Lions Gate Entertainment Corp., 2011 BCCA 228 at para. 71; and Wilson v. Alharayeri at paras. 41-42.
[117] Again, PJHL does not challenge the chambers judge's legal analysis of the oppression remedy under the Societies Act and her reliance on Dalpadado. Its complaint is with respect to the extent of the remedy granted.
[118] PJHL submits that the chambers judge's findings on procedural unfairness do not provide a basis to permanently prohibit it from taking any actions against the Surrey Knights in connection with the September 24, 2015 incident. It suggests that the reasonable expectation that is protected by the remedy granted is that no conduct of Mr. Craighead could impact on the status of the Surrey Knights within the league. PJHL says that at most, the matter should have been remitted back to the league with directions.
[119] With respect, this submission ignores the important conclusions of the chambers judge in relation to the oppression remedy. She was satisfied that the respondents had demonstrated more than minor failures. She acknowledged PJHL's authority to expel one of its Operating members. However, she found that the circumstances here involved one individual as the alleged wrongdoer who had already received a substantial penalty, and held that PJHL's obligation to act fairly and without oppression was owed to the Operating Member and not to just one of the owners. Importantly, the judge found that PJHL's treatment of the respondents "lacked probity and fair dealing", did not recognize the interests of the Surrey Knights team or Mr. Gill, and targeted only Mr. Craighead (at paras. 142-143).
[120] As the respondents point out, s. 102(2) of the Societies Act gives the court a broad discretion to grant orders "with a view to remedying or bringing to an end the matters complained of". Clearly, the judge concluded that the only way to remedy the unfairness was to bring an end to the proceedings so far as they related to the September 24, 2015 incident. The person targeted by PJHL had long been punished and in light of PJHL's persistent focus on the same individual for the same conduct, I see no error in principle in her choice of remedy.
5. Conflict/reasonable apprehension of bias
[121] Impartiality is the fundamental qualification of a judge. As the Supreme Court of Canada stated in Wewaykum Indian Band, "[t]he essence of impartiality lies in the requirement of the judge to approach the case to be adjudicated with an open mind" (at para. 58). Given its importance to our judicial process, there is a presumption of impartiality that carries considerable weight:
[59] and the law should not carelessly evoke the possibility of bias in a judge, whose authority depends upon that presumption. Thus, while the requirement of judicial impartiality is a stringent one, the burden is on the party arguing for disqualification to establish that the circumstances justify a finding that the judge must be disqualified.
[122] It is important to also recognize that judges in our society approach their task from their varied perspectives, shaped by their life experiences. While the reasonable person properly demands judges to be impartial in their judging, the same reasonable person does not expect that judges will function as "neutral ciphers": see R. v. S. (R.D.), [1997] 3 S.C.R. 484 at para. 38. This was further discussed in Yukon Francophone School Board:
[33] Judicial impartiality and neutrality do not mean that a judge must have no prior conceptions, opinions or sensibilities. Rather, they require that the judge's identity and experiences not close his or her mind to the evidence and issues. There is, in other words, a crucial difference between an open mind and empty one
[34] The reasonable apprehension of bias test recognizes that while judges "must strive for impartiality", they are not required to abandon who they are or what they know: S. (R.D.), at para. 29, per L'Heureux-Dubé and McLachlin JJ.; see also S. (R.D.), at para. 119, per Cory J. A judge's identity and experiences are an important part of who he or she is, and neither neutrality nor impartiality is inherently compromised by them. Justice is the aspirational application of law to life. Judges should be encouraged to experience, learn and understand "life" -- their own and those whose lives reflect different realities
[123] The test is whether a reasonable and informed person, with knowledge of all the relevant circumstances and viewing the matter realistically and practically, would conclude that a judge's conduct gives rise to a reasonable apprehension of bias: see Yukon Francophone School Board at para. 37, citing Miglin v. Miglin, 2003 SCC 24 at para. 26. The grounds must be substantial, and the interest of a judge cannot be "trifling" or an interest shared by every member of the community: Liszkay v. Robinson at para. 63, citing Committee of Justice and Liberty v. Canada (National Energy Board), [1978] 1 S.C.R. 369.
[124] PJHL takes no issue with the chambers judge's recognition of these legal principles, but submits that the judge discounted the impact of a dispersal draft on her son and thereby failed to recognize the real potential impacts. It contends that a reasonable, fair-minded and informed person viewing the situation objectively would have concluded that the judge was, consciously or unconsciously, motivated by the interests of her son as a player in the PJHL.
[125] The respondents point out that PJHL alleges no error of law or principle but simply argues that this issue should have been decided differently, which is not an appropriate ground of appeal.
[126] PJHL relies on the evidence of Mr. Petrachenko that was tendered in support of the application for disqualification. In his affidavit, Mr. Petrachenko responded to the question of what would happen to players on a Junior B team if a team was to dissolve:
3. There has been one occasion in my time with BC Hockey on which a member Junior team has dissolved mid-season In that case the league held a dispersal draft, so that players were distributed amongst the other teams. That would potentially lead to a ripple effect in which some of the weaker players around the league might be released from their junior teams. In the case of a Junior B team, released players would then either fall back from junior hockey to minor hockey or possibly find roster positions in Junior B leagues located elsewhere.
4. The impact on players would also be different at different times of year. If the team had dissolved in the off-season, then players would have ended up trying out for different teams. If the team had dissolved mid-season, then a dispersal draft would have more directly impacted where the players ended up
5. I note, however, that there is generally a substantial turnover from year to year amongst Junior B players. Stronger players will most often end up making a Junior A team before too long. Many players decide to focus on academics or other sports rather than playing Junior hockey for multiple seasons and play only one or two seasons of Junior B. Players who remain at the Junior B level are regularly traded.
[127] The chambers judge considered this evidence in the February 25, 2020 reasons, but could not see "how an informed, reasonable and right-minded person would think that the petition was not decided fairly", and that even unconsciously, having her son play in the league at the time when judgment was rendered affected her ability to remain impartial (at para. 53). She further explained:
[54] Stated another way, I do not see how my son playing on a PJHL team is enough to raise a reasonable apprehension of bias. In particular, I do not see how it could give rise to any interest on my part or [a] member of my family in the outcome of the petition.
[55] I make this determination having considered the size of the League, the age group of the players eligible for participation, and the substantial turnover or transitory connection of the players to their teams as described Mr. Petrachenko.
[128] I see no error in the judge's conclusion. She applied the correct legal principles and clearly understood the seriousness of her oath of office and her duty to have an open mind. I would not give effect to this ground of appeal.
Conclusion and disposition
[129] This was a difficult dispute that raised many issues of fair process. The petition extended to 755 paragraphs and included allegations of bad faith and discrimination. It is apparent that the chambers judge was exercising some restraint by addressing only two of the issues raised in the petition. She wisely recognized that establishing oppressive or unfairly prejudicial conduct did not require proof of bad faith or improper motive. However, her findings that PJHL's conduct was both oppressive and unfairly prejudicial implicitly recognized that re-litigation of a matter that has already been determined in another forum may constitute an abuse of process: see, for example, Toronto (City) v. C.U.P.E., Local 79, 2003 SCC 63. While I accept that the same act may have multiple consequences, this matter was a far cry from the circumstances in R. v. Wigglesworth, [1987] 2 S.C.R. 541, where a police officer was subject to both internal discipline and criminal sanction for one act of assault.
[130] In my view, the chambers judge made no errors in her legal or factual analysis that would undermine her conclusions or the basis for the relief granted. For all of these reasons, I would dismiss the appeal.
